breed_specific_red_flags: Lethargy in this normally tireless breed (always investigate — cancer, bloat, pain)

embedding_text: German Shorthaired Pointer. Large sporting breed, 45-70 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 9. One of the highest-energy breeds — lethargy is ALWAYS a red flag. Deep-chested = bloat/GDV risk. Prone to: bloat/GDV, hip dysplasia, cancer (hemangiosarcoma, mast cell tumors), hypothyroidism, eye conditions. Athletic injuries common. Will eat anything found outdoors. Common meds: levothyroxine, NSAIDs, joint supplements. Red flags: lethargy (always investigate), bloat symptoms, sudden collapse/pale gums, unexplained weight gain.

common_signs: bumping into furniture in dim light
reluctance to go outside at dusk

Adrenal insufficiency — vague symptoms make diagnosis difficult
Small mouth with crowded teeth increases tartar and decay

behavioral_traits.temperament: Highly intelligent, alert, trainable. Often more anxious than Standard Poodles.

behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Can be prone to anxiety and nervousness. Sensitive to household tension.

embedding_text: Miniature Poodle. Small non-sporting breed, 10-15 lbs, life expectancy 12-15 years, senior at 11. Highly intelligent — behavioral changes usually signal medical issues. Prone to: patellar luxation, PRA (progressive blindness), epilepsy, Addison's disease (vague intermittent symptoms), dental disease. Sensitive to household stress. Non-shedding coat hides skin issues. Small size makes medication dosing critical. Common meds: anti-epileptics, Addison's medications, dental care. Red flags: seizures, night vision loss, intermittent vomiting/lethargy (Addison's), behavioral changes.

embedding_text: Cane Corso. Giant working breed, 85-120 lbs, life expectancy 9-12 years, senior at 7. Giant deep-chested = very high bloat/GDV risk. Stoic and high pain tolerance — hides illness. Prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, bloat/GDV, DCM (heart disease), eye conditions (cherry eye, entropion), cruciate tears. Giant breed aging starts early. Powerful jaws increase dental injury risk. Common meds: joint supplements, NSAIDs, cardiac medications. Red flags: bloat symptoms (emergency), collapse/exercise intolerance (cardiac), any behavior change in stoic breed, sudden drooling increase.

embedding_text: Boston Terrier. Small non-sporting breed, 12-25 lbs, life expectancy 11-13 years, senior at 10. Brachycephalic — breathing difficulty, heat intolerance. Prominent eyes = extremely vulnerable to corneal ulcers (any eye injury is urgent). Prone to: brachycephalic airway syndrome, eye injuries/conditions, patellar luxation, deafness (white-headed), allergies. Flatulence and reverse sneezing are breed-normal. Common meds: eye lubricants, allergy medications. Red flags: any eye injury (urgent), blue gums/collapse in heat, worsening breathing, squinting/cloudy eye.

embedding_text: Labradoodle. Mixed/designer breed, 30-65 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 9. Prone to: hip/elbow dysplasia, Addison's disease, ear infections, EIC, eye conditions. Variable size affects risk profile. Lab food motivation = foreign body risk. Non-shedding coat hides skin issues. Common meds: Addison's medications, ear cleaners, joint supplements. Red flags: intermittent vomiting/lethargy (Addison's), exercise collapse (EIC), refusing food, chronic ear infections.

embedding_text: Persian cat. Medium brachycephalic longhair, 7-12 lbs, life expectancy 12-17 years, senior at 11. Up to 50% carry PKD gene (polycystic kidney disease). Flat face causes breathing difficulty, chronic eye issues, dental crowding, skin fold infections. Long coat requires daily grooming — mats hide skin problems. Open-mouth breathing is ALWAYS emergency (even more dangerous in flat-faced cats). Common meds: subQ fluids for kidneys, renal diet, eye lubricants. Red flags: open-mouth breathing (emergency), increased drinking (PKD), dark corneal spot, not eating 24+ hours.

embedding_text: Maine Coon cat. Large longhair, 10-25 lbs, life expectancy 12-15 years, senior at 10. Largest domestic cat breed — slow to mature (3-5 years). Genetic predisposition to HCM (heart disease) — can cause sudden death or hind leg paralysis from blood clots. Prone to: HCM, hip dysplasia (unusual for cats), SMA, PKD, dental disease. Dog-like behavior, loves water. Common meds: cardiac medications, blood clot prevention (clopidogrel), joint supplements. Red flags: sudden hind leg paralysis/cold legs (HCM blood clot — emergency), rapid/open-mouth breathing, sudden collapse.

embedding_text: Siamese cat. Medium oriental breed, 6-14 lbs, life expectancy 12-20 years, senior at 12. Most vocal cat breed — decreased vocalization signals illness. Prone to: amyloidosis (liver/kidney protein deposits), respiratory infections, asthma, GI issues (intestinal lymphoma predisposition), crossed eyes (usually cosmetic). Wool-sucking/pica is breed-associated. Sensitive to anesthesia. Extremely social — isolation causes stress. Common meds: inhaled corticosteroids for asthma, GI medications. Red flags: less vocal than normal, jaundice, chronic vomiting/weight loss, coughing/wheezing.

embedding_text: Ragdoll cat. Large semi-longhair, 10-20 lbs, life expectancy 12-17 years, senior at 10. Genetic predisposition to HCM (same gene as Maine Coons) — can cause sudden death or hind leg paralysis. Extremely docile with reduced pain response — injuries may go unnoticed. Prone to: HCM, urinary issues (FLUTD), FIP susceptibility, GI sensitivity. Slow to mature (4 years). Dog-like following behavior — withdrawal signals illness. Common meds: cardiac medications, clopidogrel, urinary diet. Red flags: hind leg paralysis/cold legs (HCM — emergency), open-mouth breathing, male straining to urinate, social withdrawal.

behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: Moderate — good jumpers despite short/absent tail.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Generally adaptable. Bonds closely with family.
notable_behaviors: Tailless gene affects spine — monitor bowel/bladder function throughout life
Bunny-hopping gait can be normal for breed OR a Manx syndrome sign — vet should differentiate
Excellent jumpers — rear legs are longer than front
Some Manx have full tails ('longies'), stumps ('stumpies'), or no tail ('rumpies') — spinal risk varies

age_specific_concerns.kitten_0_to_1: Manx syndrome assessment. Spinal evaluation. Bowel/bladder function monitoring.
age_specific_concerns.young_adult_1_to_6: Spinal health. Bowel/bladder regularity. Dental care.
age_specific_concerns.adult_6_to_10: Spinal arthritis assessment. Dental care. Weight management (protect spine).
age_specific_concerns.senior_10_plus: Spinal arthritis management. Kidney screening. Bowel/bladder function monitoring.

breed_specific_red_flags: Any bowel or bladder control changes (Manx syndrome — spinal nerve involvement)
Progressive hind leg weakness (spinal cord issues from tailless gene)
Constipation (can indicate Manx syndrome nerve involvement)
Worsening bunny-hop gait or balance issues (spinal deterioration)

common_medications.notes: If nerve involvement affects bowel function.

embedding_text: Manx cat. Medium tailless breed, 8-13 lbs, life expectancy 12-14 years, senior at 10. CRITICAL: Tailless gene can cause Manx syndrome — spinal cord defects causing incontinence, hind weakness, constipation. Severity varies from none to severe. Prone to: Manx syndrome, spina bifida, spinal arthritis, corneal dystrophy. Bunny-hop gait may be normal OR pathological. Tail length varies (rumpy/stumpy/longy) — shorter = higher spinal risk. Common meds: stool softeners, pain management, joint supplements. Red flags: bowel/bladder changes (Manx syndrome), hind leg weakness, constipation, worsening gait.

health_predispositions.description: Genetic predisposition to heart disease
White Turkish Angoras with blue eyes have very high deafness rates — hereditary
Hereditary movement disorder in some Turkish Angora lines
Common in the breed

behavioral_traits.temperament: Intelligent, playful, affectionate. Strong-willed and mischievous.
behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: Moderate to high — active and playful. Loves climbing.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Bonds strongly. Can be demanding of attention.
notable_behaviors: White/blue-eyed cats may be deaf — adjust environment accordingly
Very athletic and graceful — clumsiness is concerning
Single-layer silky coat (no undercoat) — mats less than other longhairs
Loves water — unusual for cats

age_specific_concerns.kitten_0_to_1: BAER hearing test for white/blue-eyed kittens. Ataxia screening. HCM screening.
age_specific_concerns.young_adult_1_to_6: Cardiac screening. Dental care. Hearing assessment if white.
age_specific_concerns.senior_12_plus: HCM monitoring. Kidney screening. Dental management. Arthritis assessment.

breed_specific_red_flags: New onset wobbliness or uncoordinated movement (ataxia or neurological issue)
Hind leg paralysis (HCM blood clot)
White/blue-eyed cat not responding to sounds (likely deaf — adapt environment)
Sudden clumsiness in normally graceful cat (neurological concern)

common_medications.notes: Deafness is managed environmentally — visual cues, vibration alerts, safe indoor environment.

embedding_text: Turkish Angora cat. Medium semi-longhair, 5-10 lbs, life expectancy 12-18 years, senior at 12. White/blue-eyed cats have very high deafness rates. Prone to: HCM, hereditary deafness (white/blue-eyed), hereditary ataxia, dental disease. Athletic and graceful — clumsiness always concerning. Single-layer silky coat. Loves water. Common meds: cardiac medications, dental care, environmental deafness management. Red flags: new wobbliness (ataxia), hind leg paralysis (HCM), not responding to sounds (deafness), sudden clumsiness.

health_predispositions.description: Same as Abyssinian (Somali is longhaired Abyssinian) — kidney failure from protein deposits
Genetic red blood cell disorder — shared with Abyssinian
Common and painful
Inherited progressive blindness

behavioral_traits.temperament: Extremely active, curious, social. 'The fox cat' — bushy tail and active personality.
behavioral_traits.exercise_needs: Very high — needs climbing, play, and stimulation. Athletic and agile.
behavioral_traits.anxiety_profile: Under-stimulation leads to behavioral issues. Needs companionship.
notable_behaviors: Longhaired Abyssinian — shares ALL Abyssinian health risks
Extremely active — lethargy is always concerning
Gorgeous bushy coat requires regular grooming
Very social — isolation leads to depression

age_specific_concerns.kitten_0_to_1: PKDef genetic test. PRA test. High enrichment needs.
age_specific_concerns.young_adult_1_to_6: Renal amyloidosis can present young. Dental care. Activity support.
age_specific_concerns.adult_6_to_11: Kidney monitoring. Eye exams. Dental care.
age_specific_concerns.senior_11_plus: Kidney disease screening. Vision monitoring. Dental management.

breed_specific_red_flags: Lethargy in this normally hyperactive breed (always investigate)
Increased drinking in young adult (renal amyloidosis)
Pale gums or jaundice (PKDef)
Vision changes (PRA)
embedding_text: Somali cat. Medium semi-longhair, 6-10 lbs, life expectancy 12-16 years, senior at 11. Longhaired Abyssinian — shares ALL Abyssinian health risks. Prone to: renal amyloidosis (young-onset kidney disease), PKDef (hemolytic anemia), dental disease, PRA. 'Fox cat' — extremely active, bushy tail. Lethargy always concerning. Social — isolation causes depression. Common meds: renal diet, subQ fluids, dental care. Red flags: lethargy, increased drinking in young cat, pale gums/jaundice, vision changes.
